frameworks.name: Rational Speech Act-style coordination model
frameworks.one_line: Joint probabilistic inference of threshold variable and intended sentence meaning.

frameworks.name: probabilistic pragmatic inference
frameworks.one_line: Listener-speaker coordination resolves vague positive-form adjective interpretation.

frameworks.name: degree semantics with free threshold variable
frameworks.one_line: Positive-form adjectives carry a free threshold inferred pragmatically.

core_question: How do context-sensitivity and vagueness of positive-form relative adjectives arise from pragmatic inference over a free threshold variable?
key_assumptions: Positive-form adjectives contain a free threshold variable in their semantics
Listeners jointly infer threshold and intended meaning via probabilistic coordination
Background statistical knowledge of the comparison class shapes interpretation
manipulations: Background statistical distribution of the adjective's scale (simulated)
Scale structure (open vs closed / relative vs absolute adjectives)
key_findings: Simulations show background statistics modulate inferred adjective threshold
Model reproduces correlation between scale structure and relative/absolute distinction
Model accommodates exceptions to the scale-structure generalization noted in prior work
Sorites paradox premise comes out compelling yet false on universal reading
interpretations: Vagueness and context-sensitivity emerge from threshold inference under pragmatic coordination
Probabilistic pragmatics explains the seductiveness of the sorites paradox without endorsing its premise
